What to Do When You Get in a Traffic Accident? — Litigation and Settlement (4)

Going to court isn't something anyone wants to do, but sometimes it's the only way to resolve things. So in this post I'm going to walk through litigation and settlement — what your rights are, how the process works, and what to watch out for if you end up at this stage.

I'm not a professional. This post is based on my own situation and what I learned along the way. If there's anything off, feel free to correct me. (Also note: this is based on the legal context in Taiwan.)
First, an important thing: when you get in a traffic accident, you have the right to file charges. There are generally two types of filings:
: filing for negligent injury / serious negligent injury / negligent homicide
These two are pretty different, so I'll cover them separately.
Criminal charges in a traffic accident basically boil down to these three:
: the other party, while driving, was negligent and caused you to be injured. Basically as long as someone is injured, this is on the table.
: same as above, but the injury is serious. "Serious" here has to meet the legal definition — not just what we'd casually call "serious" in everyday language.
: the other party, while driving, was negligent and caused a death.
There's also one more thing I mentioned back in "
What to Do When You Get in a Traffic Accident? — On the Scene (1)
" — hit-and-run. If you don't handle the accident properly at the scene and just take off, you'll be looking at an additional hit-and-run charge.
Most traffic accidents end up being negligent injury, which is
(告訴乃論) — meaning it's your call whether to file. If you don't file, the judicial system won't start criminal proceedings, and prosecutors can't pursue it on their own.
(非告訴乃論), where the prosecutor can pursue it without you filing. Negligent homicide is generally non-complaint-required.
After an accident, you need to watch the clock — when someone is injured, the negligent injury clock starts counting from the moment you become aware, and you have
to file. Past 6 months, you can no longer file criminal charges. In plain terms:
the 6-month countdown starts when you get the Traffic Accident Party Registration Form
Civil charges are generally filed for
. What does that mean? Basically: the other party, while driving, was negligent and caused you financial loss. Common items covered:
This is why earlier posts kept saying to keep every receipt — these become the basis for your civil filing, and the court uses them to determine the compensation amount.
How long do you have to file civil charges? Generally

I'm pulling pain and suffering out separately because a lot of people get confused here and end up just shouting out random numbers. There's no absolute standard in legal practice, but courts generally look at:
: how bad your injury is, including aftereffects (PTSD, phobias, etc.).
: how negligent the other party was. DUI, for example, is gross negligence with intent, so damages may run higher.
: if you're from a disadvantaged group, damages may be higher.
: more economically developed regions tend to result in higher damages.
: past cases for the sake of fairness.
: did they apologize, reach out, attempt settlement or mediation?

So pain and suffering is harder to pin down — you can't just shout out any number. If you want a reasonable floor for it, you really should see a psychiatrist or psychologist. They can document whether you've actually suffered psychological harm, and that gives the court real evidence to work with. (Doctors won't fake this — their own reputation is on the line.)

If you really do have to go through the filing process, don't panic. Usually you file criminal charges first because the criminal window is shorter, and civil follows after — or you can file civil as an attachment to the criminal case to resolve both at once.

The actual filing process is straightforward. When you decide to file criminal negligent injury, just go to the police agency handling your case and tell them you want to file. Bring your ID, your Traffic Accident Party Registration Form, and your hospital records (diagnosis certificate). They'll start taking your statement.

Heads up: once you file, the other party finds out immediately because the police will notify them to come in for their own statement. At that point they might start lining up a lawyer, so you should be prepared too.

If you spot that they've hired a lawyer, I'd strongly recommend you do the same. Professional matters are best handled by professionals — a lot of cases that should've been clear wins end up lost simply because the person didn't understand the law and got outmaneuvered.
To make the timing/type stuff easier to track, here's a quick reference table:
File within 2 years, must not exceed 10
A lot of people ask about the civil filing process, and the common advice is basically...
Because during the criminal process, you can attach a civil claim against the at-fault party — handling civil all in one go. That saves you time and effort.
If you really do have to go pure civil, again, I'd recommend hiring a lawyer.
If both sides are willing to settle before things go to court, that's obviously the best outcome — saves a ton of time and money. But settlement has its own pitfalls, so let me walk through them.

This is probably the most common mistake I've seen. Never meet at anyone's home or in a private space. There are way too many things that can go wrong in private settlements — you can't guarantee the other side will stay calm during the conversation. Intimidation, threats, even abduction aren't impossible, and one slip could put your life at risk.
I'd recommend a public space + bring a friend along. Good options:

: you can ask the police if you can borrow a space to discuss settlement. If anything goes wrong, it's handled on the spot. Note that police won't intervene in the settlement itself — they're just there for safety.
: cafes, convenience stores, fast food places, etc. These usually have CCTV and foot traffic, which keeps you safer.
My personal pick? The police agency. If you do reach a settlement there, you can ask the police to help close out the case and keep a copy of the settlement on file — extra protection for you.
You can find templates online easily, but a settlement doc should include:
Both parties' names and ID numbers
Description of the incident (time, location, course of events)
Both parties' rights and obligations (e.g., waiver of civil claims and no further criminal action)
Signatures and witnesses (signatures from both parties, witness signatures, contact phone numbers)
. I'd recommend three — one for the other party, one for you, and one held by a witness / police agency / mediation committee / insurance company.
If you're not sure your settlement doc is correct, get a lawyer to review it. Extra protection again.
Last but not least: if the other party in your accident is under 18, the settlement needs consent from their legal guardian to be valid. Worth noting.

Settlement is something both sides have to agree on — once you do, you can draft and sign a settlement document. The reason for putting it in writing is to make sure neither side backs out later. Getting it down in black and white matters.
